paragraphs.text:
## Stability Constant Trends Across Ligand Classes

### Fe(III): Hard Acid — Prefers Hard Donors

Fe³⁺ is a **hard Lewis acid** (high charge density, d⁵ high-spin, small radius ~0.645 Å). The stability ordering across ligand classes follows:

| Rank | Ligand Class | Representative Ligand | Thermodynamic Basis |
|------|---|---|---|
| 1 (highest) | Hexadentate aminopolycarboxylates | EDTA, CDTA, DTPA | Chelate effect (5–6 rings) + hard N/O donors; CDTA > EDTA due to cyclohexyl rigidity |
| 2 | Catecholate/phenolate | Tiron, salicylate, oxine | Hard O⁻ donors; catecholate dianion gives very high log β₃ |
| 3 | Hydroxamates | Acetohydroxamate, siderophore models | Bidentate O,O; siderophores exploit 3× chelation for log β ≈ 30 |
| 4 | Fluoride | F⁻ | Hardest halide; strong electrostatic match with Fe³⁺ |
| 5 | Dicarboxylates | Oxalate, malonate | Bidentate O,O chelates; 5-membered rings |
| 6 | Monocarboxylates | Acetate | Monodentate; no chelate effect |
| 7 | Thiocyanate | SCN⁻ | Borderline; N-bound in water; moderate stability |
| 8 | Chloride | Cl⁻ | Soft halide; poor match for hard Fe³⁺; log β₁ ≈ 1.4 |

| 1 (highest) | Hexadentate aminopolycarboxylates | EDTA, CDTA, DTPA | Chelate effect (5–6 rings) + hard N/O donors; CDTA > EDTA due to cyclohexyl rigidity |

| 3 | Hydroxamates | Acetohydroxamate, siderophore models | Bidentate O,O; siderophores exploit 3× chelation for log β ≈ 30 |
| 4 | Fluoride | F⁻ | Hardest halide; strong electrostatic match with Fe³⁺ |
| 5 | Dicarboxylates | Oxalate, malonate | Bidentate O,O chelates; 5-membered rings |
| 6 | Monocarboxylates | Acetate | Monodentate; no chelate effect |
| 7 | Thiocyanate | SCN⁻ | Borderline; N-bound in water; moderate stability |
| 8 | Chloride | Cl⁻ | Soft halide; poor match for hard Fe³⁺; log β₁ ≈ 1.4 |

paragraphs.text:
### Fe(II): Borderline Acid — Prefers N-Donors & π-Acceptors

Fe²⁺ is a **borderline-to-soft Lewis acid** (d⁶, lower charge, radius ~0.78 Å). The stability ordering:

| Rank | Ligand Class | Representative Ligand | Thermodynamic Basis |
|------|---|---|---|
| 1 (highest) | Aromatic N,N-chelates | Phen, bipy, 5-Me-phen | π-back-bonding (Fe²⁺ → π* of phen/bipy); CFSE for low-spin d⁶; log β₃ ≈ 21 for [Fe(phen)₃]²⁺ |
| 2 | Cyanide (C-donor) | CN⁻ | Strong-field π-acceptor; [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻ log β₆ ≈ 35; low-spin d⁶ maximizes CFSE |
| 3 | Hexadentate aminopolycarboxylates | DTPA, NTA | Chelate effect; moderate N/O donors |
| 4 | Aliphatic N,N-chelates | Ethylenediamine | Chelate effect; σ-only N-donor |
| 5 | Amino acids | Glycine, serine, tryptophan | Bidentate N,O; moderate stability |
| 6 | Simple N-donors | NH₃ | Monodentate; no chelate effect |
| 7 | O-donors | Citrate, phosphate | Weak match for borderline Fe²⁺ |

| 2 | Cyanide (C-donor) | CN⁻ | Strong-field π-acceptor; [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻ log β₆ ≈ 35; low-spin d⁶ maximizes CFSE |
| 3 | Hexadentate aminopolycarboxylates | DTPA, NTA | Chelate effect; moderate N/O donors |
| 4 | Aliphatic N,N-chelates | Ethylenediamine | Chelate effect; σ-only N-donor |
| 5 | Amino acids | Glycine, serine, tryptophan | Bidentate N,O; moderate stability |
| 6 | Simple N-donors | NH₃ | Monodentate; no chelate effect |
| 7 | O-donors | Citrate, phosphate | Weak match for borderline Fe²⁺ |

Fe^[3+] charge 3; Fe^[2+] charge 2 → Fe2+ has lower charge than Fe3+.

paragraphs.text:
## Thermodynamic Reasoning

### 1. Hard–Soft Acid–Base (HSAB) Theory

| Principle | Fe(III) | Fe(II) |
|---|---|---|
| HSAB classification | Hard acid | Borderline–soft acid |
| Preferred donors | Hard O (F⁻, OH⁻, catecholate, carboxylate) | Soft/borderline N (phen, bipy, CN⁻) |
| Halide preference | F⁻ ≫ Cl⁻ | Cl⁻ ≈ F⁻ (both weak) |
| Thiocyanate | Moderate (N-bound) | Moderate |

Fe³⁺'s high charge (+3) and small radius create a strong electrostatic field that stabilizes hard, electronegative donors (O, F). Fe²⁺'s lower charge and larger radius allow more covalent bonding with polarizable N-donors.

claims.text: | HSAB classification | Hard acid | Borderline–soft acid |
| Preferred donors | Hard O (F⁻, OH⁻, catecholate, carboxylate) | Soft/borderline N (phen, bipy, CN⁻) |
| Halide preference | F⁻ ≫ Cl⁻ | Cl⁻ ≈ F⁻ (both weak) |
that stabilizes hard, electronegative donors (O, F).
Fe²⁺'s lower charge and larger radius allow more covalent bonding with polarizable N-donors.

SRD-46 lists Fe3+ with O/F donors and Fe2+ with N donors, but it does not state 'preferred donors'; HSAB preference is domain knowledge.

paragraphs.text:
### 2. Crystal-Field / Ligand-Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE/LFSE)

| System | Configuration | CFSE contribution |
|---|---|---|
| Fe³⁺ (high-spin, d⁵) | t₂g³ eₘ² | Zero CFSE (half-filled d shell) |
| Fe²⁺ (high-spin, d⁶) | t₂g⁴ eₘ² | Small CFSE |
| Fe²⁺ (low-spin, d⁶) with phen/CN⁻ | t₂g⁶ | Large CFSE (2.4 Δₒ) |

This is the key **edge case**: Fe²⁺ with strong-field ligands (phen, bipy, CN⁻) undergoes a **high-spin → low-spin crossover**, gaining enormous CFSE. This explains why [Fe(phen)₃]²⁺ and [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻ are dramatically more stable than their Fe³⁺ counterparts relative to simple electrostatic prediction. Fe³⁺ (d⁵ high-spin) gains **zero** CFSE regardless of ligand field strength, so its stability is dominated by electrostatics and HSAB matching.

claims.text: Fe³⁺ (high-spin, d⁵) | t₂g³ eₘ² | Zero CFSE (half-filled d shell)
Fe²⁺ (high-spin, d⁶) | t₂g⁴ eₘ² | Small CFSE
Fe²⁺ (low-spin, d⁶) with phen/CN⁻ | t₂g⁶ | Large CFSE (2.4 Δₒ)
Fe²⁺ with strong-field ligands (phen, bipy, CN⁻) undergoes a **high-spin → low-spin crossover**
[Fe(phen)₃]²⁺ and [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻ are dramatically more stable than their Fe³⁺ counterparts
Fe³⁺ (d⁵ high-spin) gains **zero** CFSE regardless of ligand field strength
so its stability is dominated by electrostatics and HSAB matching.

paragraphs.text:
### 3. Chelate Effect

| Denticity | Example | log β enhancement |
|---|---|---|
| Monodentate | Acetate, F⁻, NH₃ | Baseline |
| Bidentate | Oxalate, en, glycine | +2–4 log units vs. monodentate analogue |
| Tetradentate | NTA | +4–6 log units |
| Hexadentate | EDTA, CDTA | +6–10 log units |
| Octadentate | DTPA | +8–12 log units |

The chelate effect is entropic: multidentate binding releases more water molecules than equivalent monodentate binding, increasing ΔS° and thus log K. CDTA > EDTA for Fe³⁺ because the rigid cyclohexyl backbone pre-organizes the donor arms (macrocyclic/preorganization effect).

claims.text: | Bidentate | Oxalate, en, glycine | +2–4 log units vs. monodentate analogue |
multidentate binding releases more water molecules than equivalent monodentate binding
increasing ΔS° and thus log K
the rigid cyclohexyl backbone pre-organizes the donor arms

paragraphs.text:
### 4. Siderophore Analogy (Hydroxamates)

The hydroxamate ligands (acetohydroxamate, threonine-, serine-, norvaline-hydroxamate) are **siderophore models**. Three bidentate O,O-hydroxamate units wrap around Fe³⁺ to give octahedral tris-chelates with log β₃ ≈ 28–30, rivaling EDTA. This is Nature's strategy for iron acquisition under aerobic conditions — hard catecholate and hydroxamate donors are perfectly matched to hard Fe³⁺.

hard catecholate and hydroxamate donors are perfectly matched to hard Fe³⁺.

paragraphs.text:
### 5. Edge Case: Fe(III)–SCN⁻

Thiocyanate (SCN⁻) is the **most-measured** Fe(III) ligand in the database (39 entries). Despite being a borderline/soft donor, it forms measurable complexes with Fe³⁺ (log β₁ ≈ 2–3), used analytically as a colorimetric test. The N-bound isomer dominates in water; the blood-red color of [Fe(SCN)]²⁺ is a classic qualitative test for Fe³⁺. Its moderate stability (far below F⁻ or catecholate) is consistent with HSAB mismatch.

The N-bound isomer dominates in water
is a classic qualitative test for Fe³⁺.

paragraphs.text:
## Summary: Stability Class Ordering

```
Fe(III) stability:  Catecholate ≈ Hydroxamate (×3) > EDTA/CDTA > Oxine > Oxalate/Malonate > F⁻ > Salicylate > NTA > Acetate > SCN⁻ > Cl⁻

Fe(II) stability:   CN⁻ (low-spin) > Phen/Bipy (low-spin) > DTPA > En/NTA > Glycine/amino acids > NH₃ > Citrate/Phosphate
```

The **crossover point** is instructive: Fe³⁺ beats Fe²⁺ for hard O-donors (oxalate, fluoride, hydroxamate) due to electrostatics, but Fe²⁺ beats Fe³⁺ for soft π-acceptors (CN⁻, phen) due to CFSE and back-bonding — a textbook illustration of how oxidation state, electronic configuration, and ligand field strength interact to determine thermodynamic stability.

---

*Source: NIST SRD-46 (Critically Selected Stability Constants of Metal Complexes). Fe(III) = metal_61 (406 ligands, 1,473 VLMs); Fe(II) = metal_62 (217 ligands, 667 VLMs).*

claims.text: Fe³⁺ beats Fe²⁺ for hard O-donors
Fe²⁺ beats Fe³⁺ for soft π-acceptors
how oxidation state, electronic configuration, and ligand field strength interact to determine thermodynamic stability
Critically Selected Stability Constants of Metal Complexes
